Spark

Stuck on abstract math? See what it's for, out among the stars.

Photo attached
Why this matters
{{ sparkResult }}

Horizon

A Schwarzschild black hole, ray-traced live. Drag to orbit, pinch or double-tap to zoom — and tap once to toss in a blob of gas.

Every pixel is a photon traced backwards through curved spacetime. The glowing arch above and below the shadow is the far side of the accretion disk — its light bends over and under the hole to reach you. The thin bright edge of the shadow is the photon ring: light that orbited the hole before escaping. One side of the disk outshines the other because that gas streams toward you at a fair fraction of light speed — relativistic doppler beaming, live. And the small star circling outside the disk is being devoured: the black hole's gravity peels a stream of gas off its surface, which spirals in, heats up, and feeds the disk — astronomers see exactly this in X-ray binary systems. The disk itself is a churning volume of gas glowing white-hot at the inner edge — millions of degrees — cooling to orange further out. And the faint blue beams firing from the poles are relativistic jets: matter flung out at near light speed by twisted magnetic fields.
This browser can't run the simulation (WebGL unavailable).
Try Safari or Chrome on a phone or computer.

Focus

Everything else can wait.

Braindump

Type everything on your mind — messy is fine.
Connect AI

RiTools runs on Google's free Gemini API. Get a free key at aistudio.google.com/apikey, then paste it here. It's saved only on this device.
Sync across devices

Your list is stored in a private GitHub Gist only you can see. Make a token with just the gist permission at github.com/settings/tokens, then paste the same token on each device.
RiTools
Enter the passcode
This device will be remembered.
